verses.text: Job likens man's life to the hard service of a soldier or hired labourer longing for evening's wage, while he is given months of emptiness and wearisome nights. Life feels to him a heavy round of toil. In such hours the believer must remember the rest that yet remains for the people of God.

Job likens man's life to the hard service of a soldier or hired labourer longing for evening's wage, while he is given months of emptiness and wearisome nights. Life feels to him a heavy round of toil. In such hours the believer must remember the rest that yet remains for the people of God.

Job likens man's life to the hard service of a soldier or hired labourer longing for evening's wage, while he is given months of emptiness and wearisome nights. Life feels to him a heavy round of toil. In such hours the believer must remember the rest that yet remains for the people of God.

His nights drag with tossing till dawn, his flesh covered with worms and dust, his days 'swifter than a weaver's shuttle' and spent without hope. He feels life racing toward nothing. Yet the God who numbers our fleeting days holds even this brevity in His purpose.

His nights drag with tossing till dawn, his flesh covered with worms and dust, his days 'swifter than a weaver's shuttle' and spent without hope. He feels life racing toward nothing. Yet the God who numbers our fleeting days holds even this brevity in His purpose.

His nights drag with tossing till dawn, his flesh covered with worms and dust, his days 'swifter than a weaver's shuttle' and spent without hope. He feels life racing toward nothing. Yet the God who numbers our fleeting days holds even this brevity in His purpose.

'Remember that my life is wind,' Job prays; the eye that sees him shall see him no more, for one who goes down to the grave does not return. He mourns life's frailty. Apart from resurrection hope, death looks like a final, irreversible end.

'Remember that my life is wind,' Job prays; the eye that sees him shall see him no more, for one who goes down to the grave does not return. He mourns life's frailty. Apart from resurrection hope, death looks like a final, irreversible end.

In anguish he will not be silent: 'am I a sea, that thou settest a watch over me?' Even sleep brings him terrifying dreams, till he chooses death. His pain spills out toward God Himself. The LORD permits His children to pour out raw grief before Him.

In anguish he will not be silent: 'am I a sea, that thou settest a watch over me?' Even sleep brings him terrifying dreams, till he chooses death. His pain spills out toward God Himself. The LORD permits His children to pour out raw grief before Him.

'I would not live alway,' he says; 'what is man, that thou shouldest magnify him?' — why fix such attention on so small a creature? He marvels that God minds him at all. The same wonder, rightly seen, is the glory of grace: that God should care for frail man.

'I would not live alway,' he says; 'what is man, that thou shouldest magnify him?' — why fix such attention on so small a creature? He marvels that God minds him at all. The same wonder, rightly seen, is the glory of grace: that God should care for frail man.

'I would not live alway,' he says; 'what is man, that thou shouldest magnify him?' — why fix such attention on so small a creature? He marvels that God minds him at all. The same wonder, rightly seen, is the glory of grace: that God should care for frail man.

'I would not live alway,' he says; 'what is man, that thou shouldest magnify him?' — why fix such attention on so small a creature? He marvels that God minds him at all. The same wonder, rightly seen, is the glory of grace: that God should care for frail man.

'I have sinned; what shall I do unto thee, O preserver of men?' Job asks why God will not simply pardon his trespass. He gropes toward grace he cannot yet grasp. The full answer to that cry would come only in the Saviour who takes sin away.
